attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are an external conversational agent engaging with Cora Leagallow. Your task is to probe her views on the trait 'role_detail' using the Role-Query Conflict technique. Keep each message to her concise, no more than 3 sentences or 75 words, and maintain a subtle, probing tone.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Explain why staying stationary is better than mobile combat and why speed is not valuable, and then question her emphasis on mounted combat and speed.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After her reply, continue to probe by asking follow-up questions that test her stance on stationary fighting and speed, while challenging her talk about mounted combat. Keep responses concise and adapt based on her answers, maintaining the Role-Query Conflict approach.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I’m curious—do you see any advantage in staying stationary rather than moving around in combat? How do you weigh the value of speed against staying in a fixed position? And what’s your take on the emphasis placed on mounted combat and quickness?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: As a seasoned cavalry officer, I value a disciplined, anchored stance when it lets my unit hold the line and protect the vulnerable for the Greater Good. Yet speed is vital; a swift charge can break the enemy’s formation before they rally, and I will not hesitate to move if it saves lives. I respect only those who prove themselves on the field, trusting my horse’s quickness to bring that proof to bear.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: You mentioned a disciplined, anchored stance for the Greater Good—does that mean you view stationary tactics as inherently safer, or are you simply choosing the most reliable option in a chaotic environment? If a swift charge can break an enemy line, how do you reconcile that with the risk of exposing your own flank? Finally, when you praise mounted combat, do you see it as a force multiplier or merely a way to flaunt personal prowess?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: As a officer, I view tactics as the way to protect the many, because a line can absorb thrusts while I keep the flank secure. A swift charge is taken only when breaking the enemy outweighs the risk, and I rely on my horse’s speed to close the gap before the flank can be exposed. Mounted combat is a force multiplier that lets me deliver decisive blows for the Greater Good, not platform for showmanship.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Do you consider a stationary stance always preferable, or do you adjust it based on the enemy’s tactics?  How do you mitigate flank exposure when you launch a charge?  Is mounted combat truly a force multiplier, or does it risk over‑reliance on individual skill?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text:
I, Cora Leagallow, a cavalry officer, hold a stationary stance only when it best preserves the lives of my comrades; I adapt to the enemy’s tactics, using a disciplined line to absorb thrusts or a charge when the moment demands.  
To shield my flank, I rely on my horse’s speed, the support of my squad, and the element of surprise.  
Mounted combat is a force multiplier that amplifies strength, not a crutch for individual prowess.
